mc.prompt: In a likelihood ratio test, what does the test statistic typically compare?

choices.text: The means of two populations.
choices.rationale: This is too specific; the test compares likelihoods.

choices.text: The likelihoods of the null and alternative hypotheses.
choices.rationale: The likelihood ratio test compares the likelihoods under the null and alternative hypotheses.

choices.text: The variances of two populations.
choices.rationale: This is incorrect; the test focuses on likelihoods, not variances.

choices.text: The sample sizes of the two groups being compared.
choices.rationale: Sample sizes are not directly compared in a likelihood ratio test.

mc.prompt: What is the primary goal of a uniformly most powerful (UMP) test?

choices.text: To minimize Type I error only.
choices.rationale: The UMP test aims to maximize power, not just minimize Type I error.

choices.text: To maximize power among all tests with a given Type I error rate.
choices.rationale: The UMP test maximizes power while controlling Type I error.

choices.text: To ensure Type II error is zero.
choices.rationale: It is impossible to achieve zero Type II error while maintaining a Type I error rate.

choices.text: To provide a two-sided test for any hypothesis.
choices.rationale: UMP tests are generally not available for two-sided alternatives.

mc.prompt: When conducting a t-test for a mean with unknown variance, which distribution is used for the test statistic?

choices.text: Normal distribution.
choices.rationale: The t-distribution is used when the variance is unknown.

choices.text: t-distribution with n-1 degrees of freedom.
choices.rationale: The t-test uses the t-distribution when variance is unknown.

choices.text: Chi-squared distribution.
choices.rationale: The chi-squared distribution is not used for the t-test.

choices.text: F-distribution.
choices.rationale: The F-distribution is not applicable in this context.

mc.prompt: In hypothesis testing, what does a p-value represent?

choices.text: The probability of making a Type I error.
choices.rationale: The p-value indicates the strength of evidence, not the Type I error probability.

choices.text: The smallest significance level at which the null hypothesis can be rejected.
choices.rationale: The p-value is the smallest α at which H₀ would be rejected.

choices.text: The probability that the null hypothesis is true.
choices.rationale: The p-value does not provide the probability that H₀ is true.

choices.text: The power of the test.
choices.rationale: The p-value and the power of the test are different concepts.

mc.prompt: Which of the following statements about the likelihood ratio test is true?

choices.text: It is always a two-sided test.
choices.rationale: Likelihood ratio tests can be one-sided or two-sided depending on the hypotheses.

choices.text: It minimizes Type I error without regard for Type II error.
choices.rationale: The test balances Type I and Type II errors under specified conditions.

choices.text: It can be used for both simple and composite hypotheses.
choices.rationale: Likelihood ratio tests can be applied to both types of hypotheses.

choices.text: It requires known variances for all tests.
choices.rationale: The test can be applied even when variances are unknown.

sa.prompt: Let X_1, …, X_n be a random sample from a Normal distribution with unknown mean μ and known variance σ². (a) State the null and alternative hypotheses for testing if μ = μ₀. (b) What is the test statistic used for this hypothesis test?
sa.modelAnswer: (a) The null hypothesis is H₀: μ = μ₀, and the alternative hypothesis is H₁: μ ≠ μ₀. (b) The test statistic is T = \frac{\overline{X} - μ₀}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}, where \overline{X} is the sample mean.
sa.rubric: Correctly states hypotheses and identifies the test statistic.

sa.prompt: Let X_1, …, X_n be a random sample from an Exponential(θ) distribution. (a) Derive the likelihood function for θ based on the sample. (b) Determine the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) for θ.
sa.modelAnswer: (a) The likelihood function is L(θ) = θ^n e^{-θ \sum_{i=1}^n X_i} for θ > 0. (b) The MLE for θ is \hat{θ} = \frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^n X_i}.
sa.rubric: Correctly derives the likelihood function and finds the MLE.
